sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency in which the umbilical cord descends below or beside the presenting part after membrane rupture, risking acute fetal hypoxia from compression. Immediate off-loading of the presenting part and rapid transfer are the priorities.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Visible or palpable cord after rupture of membranes
- Sudden fetal compromise reported by birth team or associated with obstetric history if monitored
- High-risk settings include malpresentation, prematurity, multiple pregnancy, or unengaged presenting part

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Delays in recognition and decompression can rapidly worsen fetal outcome
- Avoid unnecessary handling of the cord because this may worsen vasospasm or trauma
- If the cord is visible, note the time of presentation and keep it protected from drying and pressure
- Transport positioning should be safe for the patient and practical for maintaining decompression

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Call for urgent obstetric support and expedite transport immediately
- Avoid touching or pushing the cord back into the vagina
- Position the patient to reduce pressure on the cord, using the safest effective method available
- Keep the patient reassured, warm, and under continuous observation
- If birth is clearly imminent, prepare for delivery and neonatal resuscitation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG where clinically useful for maternal monitoring
- Gain IV access if the patient is unstable, symptomatic, or delivery appears imminent
- Consider analgesia and anti-emetic therapy within scope where this supports safe positioning and transport
- Pre-notify the receiving obstetric-capable facility early because emergency operative delivery may be required

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- A safe exaggerated lateral or knee-chest style off-loading position may help, but maternal safety during transport still matters
- If the mother is actively delivering and birth cannot be prevented, shift focus to the fastest safe birth and neonatal support
- Document the time the cord was first seen, maternal position, and transport urgency
